examples.title: NFS Mount Operation - Client Side
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how a client mounts a remote NFS share
examples.code:
PROCEDURE mount_nfs_share(serverIP, remotePath, localMountPoint)
BEGIN
 // Step 1: Create mount point directory
 IF NOT exists(localMountPoint) THEN
 CREATE_DIRECTORY(localMountPoint)
 END IF
 
 // Step 2: Request mount from NFS server
 mountRequest = CREATE_MOUNT_REQUEST(serverIP, remotePath)
 response = SEND_TO_SERVER(mountRequest)
 
 // Step 3: Validate server response
 IF response.status == SUCCESS THEN
 // Step 4: Bind remote filesystem to local point
 BIND_FILESYSTEM(response.exportHandle, localMountPoint)
 PRINT "Mount successful: " + remotePath + " mounted at " + localMountPoint
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 PRINT "Mount failed: " + response.errorMessage
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the four-step NFS mount process: creating local directory, sending mount request to server, validating response, and binding the remote file system to make it locally accessible.

examples.title: File Locking Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates how file locks prevent concurrent write conflicts
examples.code:
PROCEDURE acquire_file_lock(fileHandle, clientID, lockType)
BEGIN
 // Check if lock is available
 currentLock = LOCK_TABLE[fileHandle]
 
 IF currentLock == NULL THEN
 // No lock exists, grant new lock
 LOCK_TABLE[fileHandle] = LOCK(clientID, lockType, CURRENT_TIME)
 RETURN GRANTED
 
 ELSE IF currentLock.clientID == clientID THEN
 // Same client, upgrade lock if needed
 UPGRADE_LOCK(fileHandle, lockType)
 RETURN GRANTED
 
 ELSE
 // Different client holds lock
 IF lockType == NON_BLOCKING THEN
 RETURN DENIED
 ELSE
 // Add to wait queue
 ADD_TO_QUEUE(fileHandle, clientID, lockType)
 RETURN QUEUED
 END IF
 END IF
END

PROCEDURE release_file_lock(fileHandle, clientID)
BEGIN
 IF LOCK_TABLE[fileHandle].clientID == clientID THEN
 FREE_LOCK(fileHandle)
 // Grant lock to next waiting client
 nextClient = GET_FROM_QUEUE(fileHandle)
 IF nextClient != NULL THEN
 GRANT_LOCK(fileHandle, nextClient)
 END IF
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This code shows a lock manager implementation with lock acquisition and release. It handles lock conflicts, queuing for blocked requests, and granting locks to waiting clients when resources become available.

examples.title: File Caching in Distributed System
examples.description: Simplified client-side cache management for remote file access
examples.code:
PROCEDURE read_file_with_cache(filePath, clientID)
BEGIN
 // Check if file is in local cache
 cacheEntry = FILE_CACHE[filePath]
 
 IF cacheEntry != NULL AND cacheEntry.isValid THEN
 // Cache hit - return cached data
 IF NOT cacheEntry.isExpired() THEN
 PRINT "Cache hit for: " + filePath
 RETURN cacheEntry.data
 END IF
 END IF
 
 // Cache miss - fetch from server
 PRINT "Cache miss - requesting from server"
 serverData = REQUEST_FROM_SERVER(filePath, clientID)
 
 // Update cache with new data
 cacheEntry = CACHE_ENTRY(serverData, CURRENT_TIME, CACHE_TIMEOUT)
 FILE_CACHE[filePath] = cacheEntry
 
 RETURN serverData
END

PROCEDURE write_file_with_cache(filePath, data, clientID)
BEGIN
 // Write-through caching strategy
 // First write to server
 serverResponse = WRITE_TO_SERVER(filePath, data, clientID)
 
 IF serverResponse.status == SUCCESS THEN
 // Update local cache
 FILE_CACHE[filePath] = CACHE_ENTRY(data, CURRENT_TIME, CACHE_TIMEOUT)
 RETURN SUCCESS
 ELSE
 RETURN FAILED
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: Demonstrates cache management with read and write operations. Read uses copy-on-read caching, while write uses write-through strategy ensuring server data consistency.
